```{=latex}
\setstretch{1.5}
```

# Summary {.unnumbered .unlisted}

**Title:** *Heterogeneity in GLP-1, GIP and glucagon responses during an oral glucose tolerance test among individuals at high risk of type 2 diabetes: The ADDITION-PRO study*

**Introduction:** Current risk stratification among individuals at high risk of type 2 diabetes relies on glycemic classification, although this may not capture underlying metabolic heterogeneity. Postprandial incretin and glucagon responses may provide insight into this heterogeneity. Body fat distribution is another dimension of metabolic risk, but its association with hormonal responses remains unclear. This study aimed to identify metabolic subgroups based on OGTT-derived measures of glucose and hormonal responses and to examine their associations with body fat distribution.

**Research design and methods:** K-means clustering was applied to 1,381 individuals at high risk of type 2 diabetes from the ADDITION-PRO cohort. Clustering variables included OGTT-derived measures of glucose, incretins (glucagon-like peptide-1 and glucose-dependent insulinotropic polypeptide), insulin sensitivity and glucagon suppression. Visceral (VAT) and subcutaneous (SAT) adipose tissue and body fat percentage were assessed by ultrasonography and bioelectrical impedance analysis. Associations with standardized measures of fat distribution were assessed using linear regression models adjusted for age, sex, smoking and physical activity.

**Results:** Three clusters were identified. Cluster 3 was characterized by the highest glucose levels, lowest insulin sensitivity, highest VAT, SAT, VAT/SAT ratio and body fat percentage (0.68, 0.28, 0.18 and 0.63 SD units higher than cluster 2, respectively). Clusters 1 and 2 had similar glycemic profiles but differed in incretin secretion and glucagon suppression; cluster 1 had the lowest incretin secretion but highest glucagon suppression, while cluster 2 showed the opposite pattern. Cluster 1 had similar VAT to cluster 2 but higher SAT (0.25 SD units) and body fat percentage (0.15 SD units), and a lower VAT/SAT ratio (-0.23 SD units).

**Conclusion:** Three subgroups with distinct incretin and glucagon responses were identified, and these also differed in body fat distribution, particularly in VAT and SAT. Given the cross-sectional design, future studies should examine whether this heterogeneity predicts risk trajectories for type 2 diabetes and responses to prevention and treatment strategies.
